turns.content: I just finished my bootcamp and landed a job, but I'm stuck on this enum thing in our new crate. We have an Enum defined as pub enum Status { Idle, Running } and we want to add a new variant later without breaking existing code that matches against it.

That is exactly the problem #[non_exhaustive] solves for library evolution. If you mark your public enum with #[non_exhaustive], any downstream crate using this type must use wildcard arms like match status { VariantA => ..., _ => ... } instead of exhaustive patterns.
So if our internal team adds a new variant, they won't need to bump the version number? But what about the structs inside enums?

You are right that #[non_exhaustive] prevents breaking changes when adding variants. However, #[non_exhaustive] on struct fields works differently; it means new fields can be added without changing the type's size or layout, which is useful for serialization crates like serde_json::Value.
Wait, so if I apply #[non_exhaustive] to my enum and a downstream crate tries to match on it with specific arms, does that error out immediately?
Yes, the compiler will explicitly refuse non-wildcard patterns. The downstream code must be written to expect unknown variants because #[non_exhaustive] signals that the type can grow externally.
Got it, so in my current crate where we match on this enum, I don't need wildcard arms right? Since we control both sides of the boundary?

Correct. The #[non_exhaustive] constraint only applies to downstream crates that import your type but not your source code. Inside your own crate, you can use exhaustive match patterns without issues.
This makes sense, but I was worried about the semver implications. Does this mean we never have to bump minor versions for adding variants?

Adding a variant with #[non_exhaustive] is technically a non-breaking change from the perspective of existing users who must adopt wildcard arms. However, you still need to ensure your API documentation clearly states that new variants may arrive unexpectedly.

This looks like a dynamic switch statement, but Rust enums feel static. I thought enums were just fixed options like the ones in my old C++ code where you couldn't add new values without recompiling everything.

That's exactly the problem that #[non_exhaustive] solves. Think of it as telling downstream crates, 'these variants might change.' When a crate uses this attribute on an enum or struct, they must use wildcard arms in match expressions instead of listing every possible variant explicitly.
So if my library adds a new variant tomorrow, any client code using my API breaks?

Not necessarily. If you mark the enum with #[non_exhaustive], clients are forced to use wildcard arms like `match value { Some(_) => ..., None(_) => ... }`. This prevents compile errors when you add variants, but it does mean your API surface changes subtly even if the behavior stays the same.
I'm using serde_json::Value to serialize data and I want to keep adding variants without breaking things. Can #[non_exhaustive] help with that?

Yes, but you need to be careful. #[non_exhaustive] on an enum allows downstream crates to match against it safely, yet serde_json::Value itself is already dynamic by design. You should apply #[non_exhaustive] only where you control the definition and want to signal future changes.
What if I put #[non_exhaustive] on a struct field instead? Does that behave differently for match expressions?

Putting #[non_exhaustive] on a struct field tells the compiler that this field might change or be opaque to external crates. However, match behavior depends more on whether the enum itself is marked #[non_exhaustive]. Field-level annotations are mostly for internal consistency and documentation clarity.
I tried adding a variant in my crate and got an error saying I need to update all match arms. Is there any way to avoid this?

If you don't mark your enum with #[non_exhaustive], then yes, every downstream consumer must list all variants explicitly. To allow future growth without breaking consumers, the convention is to use #[non_exhaustive] on public enums so they can evolve safely.
Can I mix #[non_exhaustive] with #[allow(dead_code)] or other attributes?

Yes, you can combine them. But remember, #[non_exhaustive] is primarily about preventing breakage from adding variants. It doesn't interact directly with linting attributes like #[allow(dead_code)], though both affect how your code compiles.
Okay so if I use #[non_exhaustive] on my main enum, clients can safely add new fields and won't get match errors. That's an aha moment!

Exactly. Now you have backward compatibility built in. Just ensure your public API doesn't expose internal details that could be misinterpreted, but #[non_exhaustive] gives you the freedom to evolve your enum definitions without forcing consumers to update their match statements.

I'm getting a compiler error when I try to add a new variant to an enum in my downstream crate. The message says one of the arms in this match is unreachable, and it's complaining about #[non_exhaustive] on the enum definition. What went wrong?

That error happens because #[non_exhaustive] tells the compiler that new variants might be added later by other crates, so you cannot list every possible variant in a match statement unless you are inside the same crate as the definition. If your downstream crate is not the one defining the enum, you must use wildcard arms like match value { Some(_) | None => ... } to accept any future addition without breaking compilation.

Wait, I thought #[non_exhaustive] only applied when the variant is marked non_exhaustive in my own crate. My downstream crate uses this enum but defines its own match arms expecting all variants to be explicit. Why does it still complain?

You're mixing up two concepts: #[non_exhaustive] on the enum itself versus #[non_exhaustive] on struct fields within that enum. When you mark an entire enum as #[non_exhaustive], every arm in any matching pattern must be exhaustive over all possible variants, even if they come from external crates. The compiler enforces this because it cannot guarantee your downstream crate knows about future variants unless you use wildcard arms.
So if I just add #[non_exhaustive] to the struct fields inside my enum, that should fix it? I want to keep explicit arms for common cases but allow new fields later without breaking downstream users.

You're confusing field-level #[non_exhaustive] with variant-level #[non_exhaustive]. Field-level #[non_exhaustive] allows adding new fields to a struct without breaking existing code, but it doesn't affect match exhaustiveness. If you want downstream crates to accept arbitrary future variants in their match arms, the enum itself must be marked #[non_exhaustive], and then all matching patterns must use wildcard arms like Some(_) | None => ...
Okay, I see. But what if my downstream crate wants to handle specific variants explicitly? Can I have some explicit arms in match while still being #[non_exhaustive]?

No, not really. If an enum is marked #[non_exhaustive], then any match against it must be exhaustive using wildcard arms. You cannot mix explicit and wildcard arms unless you're in the same crate as the definition. For downstream crates, you have to use wildcard arms entirely to remain compatible with future variants.
That defeats my goal of clear error messages when someone uses an invalid variant. I want to catch mistakes early in downstream crates, not just silently ignore them with wildcards.

You're right that wildcard arms hide potential bugs, but there's a better approach: keep the enum #[non_exhaustive] for API stability while documenting clearly in your crate's docs that downstream crates must use wildcard arms. Then if someone tries to write an exhaustive match with specific variants, they'll get a clear compiler error telling them to switch to wildcards instead of silently ignoring invalid future variants.
Got it. So the pattern is: mark enum #[non_exhaustive], document that downstream needs wildcard arms, and let the compiler enforce this rule rather than trying to manually list all possible variants?

Exactly. That's how you maintain backward compatibility while allowing evolution. The #[non_exhaustive] attribute ensures that adding new variants later won't break downstream crates, provided they adapt their match patterns to use wildcards. This is the standard way to design enums in Rust libraries where future extensibility matters.
One last thing: what if I need #[non_exhaustive] on a struct field too? Does that interact with the enum's #[non_exhaustive] attribute?

Yes, they work together but serve different purposes. Field-level #[non_exhaustive] allows adding new fields to structs without breaking existing code, while enum-level #[non_exhaustive] prevents exhaustive match patterns in downstream crates. You can use both: mark struct fields as #[non_exhaustive] for internal flexibility and the entire enum as #[non_exhaustive] for external API stability.

I'm working with a new Rust library, and I'm trying to understand how enums with #[non_exhaustive] work. Specifically, if I add variants later on without breaking downstream crates that use wildcard arms in their match statements, is that safe?

Good question! Using `#[non_exhaustive]` on enums in your crate signals to downstream users that you might extend the enum in future versions. This is a way of maintaining backwards compatibility while allowing for evolution. If you add variants without breaking changes, it’s usually safe if the new variants don’t conflict with existing identifiers.
So, say I have an enum like this in my crate: `enum Status { Active, Inactive }` and downstream crates match on it using `_ => {}`. Can I add a variant later without breaking them?
Yes, that should work. The wildcard arm `_ => {}` will catch any new variants you add in future versions as long as the discriminant values don’t overlap.
What about struct fields? Can I mark a struct field with #[non_exhaustive]?

Good point. Actually, `#[non_exhaustive]` is primarily for enums and variants, not individual fields in structs. If you want to prevent users from relying on a specific internal representation, you might want to reconsider your API design or use private fields with public getters instead.
Okay, so if I have an enum like `enum Status { Active, Inactive }` and downstream crates match on it using `_ => {}`, can I add a variant later without breaking them?
Yes, that’s safe. The wildcard arm `_ => {}` will handle any new variants you add in future versions.
